title: Anomalous Mixed-State Floquet Topology in One-Dimensional Open Quantum Systems
authors: Görkem D. Dinc, Alexander Schnell, Andy M. Martin
abstract: We investigate the non-equilibrium topology of a periodically driven, dissipative Su-Schrieffer-Heeger chain using the ensemble geometric phase (EGP) $φ_{\mathrm{EGP}}$-a generalisation of the Zak phase to open quantum systems. In contrast to earlier work, we use Floquet-Born-Markov theory to describe the coupling to thermal reservoirs microscopically. We show that the steady state can be characterised by a Hermitian purity spectrum, providing a direct analogue of band topology for mixed states. The periodic drive induces nontrivial winding and a quasienergy spectrum with distinct $0$ and $π$ band gaps, with protected edge modes in each gap. We identify a pair of topological invariants $(φ^{0}_{\mathrm{EGP}}, Δφ^π_{\mathrm{EGP}})$, revealing a structure consistent with a $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$ classification known from isolated Floquet SSH systems, and show how it extends to a dissipative, finite-temperature setting in regimes where the steady-state structure remains well defined. Our results demonstrate when and how known Floquet topology survives in a driven-dissipative Gaussian steady state and establish Floquet topology as a robust concept beyond isolated zero-temperature systems. The underlying formalism provides a general framework for quadratic fermionic systems with linear bath couplings.
summary: This paper explores how topology survives in quantum systems that are both periodically driven and subject to dissipation. By studying a driven SSH chain, the authors show that the complex topological structure (Z x Z) found in isolated systems remains intact even when the system is coupled to thermal baths. They introduce the Ensemble Geometric Phase as a way to track these topological features in mixed states. The results demonstrate that topological edge modes can be protected in both the 0 and pi quasienergy gaps of the steady state.
main_problem: Investigating whether the topological classification of isolated Floquet systems persists in driven-dissipative, mixed-state settings at finite temperatures.
main_result: The study establishes that a Z x Z topological classification exists for the driven-dissipative SSH chain, characterized by a pair of invariants associated with the 0 and pi quasienergy gaps.
method: The authors use Floquet-Born-Markov theory to model system-bath coupling and the Ensemble Geometric Phase (EGP) to generalize topological invariants to mixed states.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional, periodically driven Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) chain of Majorana fermions coupled to thermal reservoirs.
key_observables: Ensemble Geometric Phase (EGP), purity spectrum, quasienergy spectrum, and Liouvillian rapidity spectrum.
important_parameters: Driving frequency (omega), driving strength (V), hopping amplitudes (t, t'), and reservoir temperature.
main_assumptions: The system is treated within the Born-Markov approximation (weak coupling) and the steady state is assumed to be a Gaussian quadratic state.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the SSH model geometry, the formation of Floquet replicas and edge states in the quasienergy spectrum, the purity spectrum gaps, and the winding of the EGP in the complex plane.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the driven-dissipative SSH model, develops the Floquet-Born-Markov framework for Gaussian states, defines the Ensemble Geometric Phase, and then applies these tools to identify topological invariants and phase transitions.
why_interesting: It provides a robust theoretical framework for extending Floquet topology from isolated, zero-temperature systems to realistic, dissipative, and finite-temperature non-equilibrium steady states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Sketch of the SSH model. In red, we highlight a unit cell consisting of two sites, A and B, connected by bonds indicating the intracell and intercell nearest-neighbour hop- pings. (a) Fixed intracell and intercell hoppings t and t′, respectively, and (b) time-periodically modulated hoppings τ(t) = t + 2V cos(ωt) (intracell) and τ ′(t) = t′ −2V cos(ωt) (intercell). Here, V and ω denote the driving strength and frequency, respectively.

FIG. 2. Extended OBC quasienergy spectra εµT of the periodically driven SSH Hamiltonian HPDSSH(t) in Eq. (9), showing the zeroth and first two (±2) Floquet replicas as a function of driving frequency ω for a chain length L = 40, a driving strength of V = −0.2 and tunneling amplitudes t = 0.8, t′ = 1.3. Due to the periodic modulation, each Flo- quet replica exhibits a gap at εµT = 0 (mod 2π) (0 gap) and another at εµT = π (mod 2π) (π gap), allowing topological edge states in either gap.

FIG. 3. Sketch of the system-bath configuration of interest in this work. Sublattice A (B) of the SSH chain is coupled dissipatively to a Markovian fermionic thermal bath RA (RB) at inverse temperature βA (βB) and chemical potential µA (µB). Here, the tunnelling amplitudes τ(t) and τ ′(t) are time- periodically modulated with driving frequency ω and driving amplitude V , as in Fig. 1(b). The drive is chosen to preserve the chiral symmetry of the system.

FIG. 4. Schematic purity spectrum λj of a Gaussian mixed state as a function of crystal momentum k, exhibiting purity bands |uk,j⟩and a finite purity gap ∆Gap between the lowest bands, required for the well-definedness of topological invari- ants like the EGP ϕEGP.

FIG. 5. Behaviour of the steady-state correlations between Majorana sites in a driven and undriven dissipative SSH chain. (a-c) Correlation matrix WNESS(0) −1 = ℑ[CNESS(0)] evaluated at time t = 0T for a chain of length L = 6, with hopping amplitudes t = 1.3, t′ = 0.8, chemical potentials µA = µB = 0, inverse temperatures βA = βB ≡β = 2, dissipation strength λ = 0.05, density of states gA,c = gB,c ≡gc = 0.01, and driving strength V = −0.3, for ω = 0.4 (a), ω = 0.8 (b), and ω = 1.2 (c). (d) WNESS(t) −1 for the undriven case V = 0. (e) WNESS(t) −1 for ω = 0.8 and t ∈[0T, 0.25T, 0.5T, 0.75T], illustrating the effect of micromotion.

FIG. 6. Liouvillian rapidity spectrum {βj} of the driven dis- sipative SSH chain. We show ℜ{βj} (a) and ℑ{βj} (c) of the OBC rapidity spectrum for a chain length of L = 20, system parameters as in Fig. 5, and reservoir density of states g(E) as in Eq. (46) with gc = 0.01, as a function of the driving fre- quency ω. To calculate the Floquet-Born-Markov rates, we cut off the Floquet spectrum at |Kmax| = 3. Note that (b) is the zoomed-in view of (a).

FIG. 7. OBC purity spectra of a driven dissipative SSH chain. Purity spectrum {λF j } obtained from the steady-state covari- ance matrix in the Floquet basis CF NESS for a chain length of L = 20 (a), and the same system parameters as in Fig. 6, as a function of ω. We observe a similar (quasi-)band structure as in Fig. 6(c), with a finite purity gap at λ = 0 and, due to the Floquet drive, additional finite (±π) purity gaps opening up. The purity spectra {λj} obtained from the steady-state covariance matrix CNESS(tf), evaluated at fixed time tf = 0, is shown in (b).

FIG. 8. Finite temperature topological phase transitions in a driven dissipative SSH chain. Quasienergy spectrum {εµ} as a function of the driving frequency ω, calculated from HMajo, for a chain length of L = 50 unit cells, a driving strength V = −0.3 and tunneling amplitudes t = 0.8, t′ = 1.3 (a) and t = 1.3, t′ = 0.8 (b). (c) Floquet basis purity spectrum {λF j }, calculated for L = 20, and corresponding pair of topological invariants (ϕ0 EGP, ∆ϕπ EGP) (e), calculated for L = 6, as a function of ω, for the same parameters as in Fig. 6. FIG. 9. (a-c) Onto the lower Floquet band projected EGP ϕπ EGP(t) = arg (Zπ(t)), obtained from Cπ NESS(t), plotted over t within a period t ∈[0, T], for a chain length of L = 6, OBC and the same system parameters as in Fig. 6, for ω = 0.40 (a), ω = 0.80 (b) and ω = 1.2 (c). The plots show that ϕπ EGP(t) itself is not quantised but strongly indicate that accumulated differential changes in the EGP ∆ϕπ EGP within a period T are.
